does: This paper conducts a mechanistic interpretability study of GPT-2 to understand how sentiment information flows through its layers. Using activation patching experiments across all 12 transformer layers, the authors test whether sentiment processing follows a two-stage architecture with early lexical detection and mid-layer contextual integration. They confirm that early layers (0-3) function as lexical sentiment detectors, encoding position-specific polarity signals independently of context. However, they falsify the hypothesis that contextual phenomena like negation, sarcasm, and domain shifts are processed in specialized middle layers. Instead, their causal analysis reveals that contextual integration happens primarily in late layers (8-11) through a unified, non-modular mechanism rather than phenomenon-specific submodules.
connects: This work extends prior mechanistic interpretability research on transformer models by applying causal intervention techniques to understand sentiment processing specifically. It builds on earlier findings suggesting hierarchical linguistic processing in neural networks and responds to hypotheses about modular specialization in transformer layers. The paper connects to broader work on feature learning and distributed representations in language models, while also engaging with interpretability methodologies like activation patching that have been developed to probe internal model computations. It addresses outstanding questions about whether language models process linguistic phenomena through dedicated specialized circuits or more general mechanisms.
enables: These findings open directions for more detailed mechanistic studies of how transformers integrate contextual information in late layers. The results suggest future work should focus on characterizing the unified contextual integration mechanism rather than seeking modular circuits, potentially revealing new architectural principles in large language models. The paper enables improved mechanistic interpretability frameworks that account for late-layer integration of complex linguistic phenomena. It also has implications for model editing, pruning, and control methods that might target late layers for sentiment manipulation. More broadly, it demonstrates causal methodology for understanding black-box language model behavior, providing templates for similar studies on other linguistic and cognitive phenomena across transformer architectures.
